title: Sesame halva
subtitle: Middle Eastern sesame seed sweet with a crumbly, fudgy texture
excerpt: Halva made from tahini and a hot sugar syrup cooked to soft-ball stage, beaten together until the mixture seizes into a dense, fudgy-crumbly confection. The texture is unlike any other sweet: dry on the tongue, rich with sesame, with a melt that reveals the underlying flavour gradually. Made in a loaf tin and sliced.
sourceNotes: Sesame halva is documented across Middle Eastern, Eastern European Jewish, and Greek confectionery traditions. The tahini-and-sugar-syrup method appears in confectionery manuals from the late nineteenth century onwards in multiple traditions. Greek and Israeli versions of sesame halva using tahini and a sugar or honey syrup are widely documented. The recipe here follows the established formula for tahini-based halva using a cooked sugar syrup, consistent with sources from the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze sliced, wrapped in baking paper. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for two weeks at room temperature, wrapped, or up to a month in the fridge. Improves over the first two days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven required. The sugar syrup is cooked to soft-ball stage at 121°C. A sugar thermometer is essential — the texture of the finished halva depends on reaching this temperature accurately.

glossaryTerms.definition: The characteristic crumbly-fudgy texture of halva is determined by how thoroughly the hot sugar syrup and tahini are combined. When the syrup is poured into the tahini and beaten, the mixture seizes rapidly — going from liquid to a thick, grainy paste within a minute or two. Beating longer produces a smoother, more fudgy texture; stopping earlier produces a more crumbly, flaky result. Most halva sits between the two. Pour the mixture into the tin the moment it becomes too thick to pour smoothly — once it seizes fully in the bowl, it cannot be reshaped.

content.text: forms quickly and without warning. Have the lined tin, the tahini, and all equipment ready before cooking the syrup. If hot syrup touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.

Line a small loaf tin (about 450 g capacity) with baking paper. Place the tahini, vanilla, and salt in a large heatproof mixing bowl and stir together — the tahini should be at room temperature and liquid enough to pour. Combine the sugar and water in a small saucepan and bring to the boil over medium heat without stirring. Clip the sugar thermometer to the pan and cook, without stirring, until the syrup reaches 121°C.

Remove from the heat. If hot syrup touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed. Pour the syrup into the tahini in a steady stream, stirring constantly with a wooden spoon or heatproof spatula. The mixture will begin to thicken and lose its glossy sheen within a minute. Keep stirring until it becomes too thick to pour smoothly — it should still just about flow. Pour immediately into the lined tin and smooth the top.

Leave at room temperature to cool for 1 hour, then refrigerate for at least 2 hours until firm. Turn out and slice with a thin, sharp knife — the halva will crumble slightly at the cut edge, which is correct. Serve at room temperature for the fullest flavour.
